request.question: Who is able to fill a larger variety of roles in the entertainment industry, Dave Moody or Ransom Riggs?

title: Ransom Riggs
Library of Souls
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
Dave Moody
Hollow City (novel)
Season of Miracles
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children
Entertainment industry in India
The Moody Brothers
Praise Band: The Movie
sentences: Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
Library of Souls is a sequel to 2014 novel "Hollow City" written by Ransom Riggs and third book in the series of "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".
It was released on September 22, 2015 by Quirk Books.
No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.
It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.
David B. Moody is an artist, producer, songwriter and filmmaker from North Carolina.
His instrumental proficiency has earned him two Grammy Award Nominations and three International CMA Awards as a member of The Moody Brothers.

After becoming regular performers on the Grand Ole Opry and touring extensively throughout Europe in the 80s, the trio of Carolina brothers signed a contract with the Walt Disney Company in 1992 to open and perform their own nightly concerts at Disneyland Paris' Disney Village, where they performed for over 50 million guests during their time in France.
Hollow City is a 2014 dark fantasy novel and a sequel to "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children" written by Ransom Riggs.
It was released on January 14, 2014 by Quirk Books.
The novel is set right after the first, and sees Jacob and his friends fleeing from Miss Peregrine's to the "peculiar capital of the world", London.
Season of Miracles is a 2013 American Christian-themed dramatic film directed by Dave Moody and starring John Schneider, Grayson Russell, Andrew Williams, and Nancy Stafford.

Based on writer Rusty Whitener's award winning novel "A Season of Miracles", the story is set in Alabama during the 1970s and follows a group of twelve-year-olds and their championship little league baseball season.
Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children is a contemporary fantasy debut novel by American author Ransom Riggs.
The story is told through a combination of narrative and vernacular photographs from the personal archives of collectors listed by the author.
The media and entertainment industry in India consists of many different segments under its folds such as television, print, and films.
It also includes smaller segments like radio, music, OOH, animation, gaming and visual effects (VFX) and Internet advertising.
Entertainment industry in India has registered an explosive growth in last two decades making it one of the fastest growing industries in India.
From a single state owned channel, Doordarshan in the 1990s there are more than 400 active channels in the country.
Worldwide, 2010 saw the global economy begin to recover from a steep decline in 2009.
Improved economic conditions in 2010 played a major role in a rebound in customer spend.
Since the world economy begin to recover from the global financial crisis of 2008, improved economic conditions played a major role in rebound in consumer spend.
While India was not critically impacted by the downturn in 2008 and 2009, it demonstrated one of the highest growth rates this year and continued to at a healthy pace.
The rising rate of investments by the private sector and foreign media and entertainment (M&E) majors have improved India's entertainment infrastructure to a great extent.
As per the recent report by PricewaterhouseCoopers (PwC), Indians are likely to spend more on entertainment in the coming years with a steady growth in their disposable income.
And as per the combined survey report by KMPG and FICCI, the entertainment industry in India is expected to expand by 12.5% every year and is likely to reach US$20.09 billion by the year 2013.
The industry pegged at INR 5808 billion in 2009 as compared to INR 3565 billion in 2005.

The Indian Media & Entertainment Industry grew from US$12.9 billion in 2009 to US$14.4 billion in 2010, a growth of 11 per cent, according to a report by the Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FICCI) and research firm KPMG.
The report also states that backed by positive industry sentiment and growing media consumption, the industry is estimated to achieve growth of 13 per cent in 2011 to touch US$16.2 billion.
As the industry braces for exciting times ahead, the sector is projected to grow at a CAGR of 14 percent to reach US$28.1 billion by 2015.

The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".
This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.
Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.
It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.
The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Who is able to fill a larger variety of roles in the entertainment industry, Dave Moody or Ransom Riggs?
hop1_docs: Ransom Riggs | Ransom Riggs (born February 3, 1979) is an American writer and filmmaker best known for the book "Miss Peregrine's Home for Peculiar Children".

No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School | No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School is a 2010 direct-to-video family comedy film.  It starred Bill Cobbs, Lee Meriwether, Blake Michael, Celeste Kellogg and Ashton Harrell.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was directed by Dave Moody for Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures, who also provided the musical score for the film.

Praise Band: The Movie | Praise Band: The Movie is a 2008 faith-based film by Elevating Entertainment Motion Pictures.  It starred George Hamilton IV, Adam Melton and James Dana Bryan.  The screenplay was written by Joshua Moody and the film was produced and directed by Dave Moody.

The Moody Brothers | The Moody Brothers are an Americana country music trio who gained prominence in 1985 when they were nominated for a Grammy Award for their instrumental performance of the classic fiddle tune "Cotton Eyed Joe".  This trio of brothers, Carlton Moody, Dave Moody, and Trent Moody were nominated for a second Grammy in 1990 and won three International Country Music Awards in Europe.

Dave Moody (sportscaster) | David Wayne "Dave" Moody is an American motorsports commentator who works for the Motor Racing Network.  He is the lead turn announcer for MRN and has worked for the radio network on and off since 1983.  He moved to MRN full-time in 1997 and became the lead turn announcer in 2001.  Since 2003, Moody has been the host of the nationally syndicated Sirius XM show "Sirius XM Speedway" on Sirius XM NASCAR Radio.
Dave Nutting | David Judd Nutting is an industrial design engineer who played a role in the early video game industry.  He is a graduate of the Pratt Institute with a degree in industrial design.

Ransomes Industrial Estate | Ransomes Industrial Estate, also known as Ransomes Europark is combined retail and business park on the South-Eastern edge of Ipswich in Suffolk, UK.  It takes its name from Ransomes, Sims & Jefferies, which still maintains a presence on the park.
hop1_titles: No Limit Kids: Much Ado About Middle School
response.summary_1:
Facts from passages:
- [1] Ransom Riggs: "is an American writer and filmmaker" (passage 1).
- [2] Dave Moody: film "was directed by Dave Moody" (passage 2).
- [2] Dave Moody: he "also provided the musical score" for that film (passage 2).
- [3] Dave Moody: that film "was produced and directed by Dave Moody" (passage 3).
- [4] Dave Moody: member of The Moody Brothers (an "Americana country music trio") — Dave Moody is a musician (passage 4).
- [5] Dave Moody (different): "David Wayne 'Dave' Moody is an American motorsports commentator" — a sportscaster (passage 5).

Bridge entities & ambiguities preventing a final answer:
- "Dave Moody" is ambiguous in the passages: (A) Dave Moody — musician/filmmaker associated with Elevating Entertainment and The Moody Brothers, and (B) Dave Moody — motorsports commentator. Do not assume which Dave Moody the question intends.
- Missing/unconfirmed facts for comparison: full, authoritative lists of occupations/credited roles for (1) the entertainment Dave Moody (e.g., director, producer, composer, musician, songwriter, actor?) and (2) Ransom Riggs (beyond "writer and filmmaker": does he also direct, produce, screenwrite, act, photograph, etc.). These comprehensive occupation/credit lists are needed to count and compare "variety of roles in the entertainment industry."

Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve ambiguity and missing facts:
1) Fetch "Dave Moody" disambiguation page (or search results) — extract the list of Dave Moody entries and canonical page titles to identify distinct individuals (confirm which page is the entertainment Dave Moody vs. the sportscaster).
2) Fetch the entertainment Dave Moody page(s):
   - Page(s) to fetch: "Dave Moody" (entertainment variant) or "Dave Moody (film director/musician)" and/or "The Moody Brothers".
   - Sections/fields to extract: infobox "Occupation", "Career" or "Filmography" and "Discography" sections, list of film credits with roles (director, producer, composer, screenwriter), and membership/credits with The Moody Brothers.
3) Fetch the full "Ransom Riggs" page (passage 1 is present but minimal):
   - Sections/fields to extract: infobox "Occupation", "Career"/"Filmography"/"Bibliography" sections, and any listed film credits or production/screenwriting/directing roles.
4) (If needed) Fetch "Dave Moody (sportscaster)" page to confirm that this is a separate individual and should not be used for the entertainment-role count (passage 5 is present but fetching will confirm).

No final answer provided because required occupation/credit lists and Dave Moody disambiguation are missing or ambiguous in the supplied passages.
